name: Gion Matsuri
best_for: Experiencing Japan's grandest traditional festival
travel_tip: Book hotels 3+ months in advance. July 17 (Saki Matsuri parade) is the main event. Evening stalls on July 15-16 are magical.

name: Nebuta Matsuri
best_for: Witnessing massive illuminated art floats parading through streets at night
travel_tip: Rent a haneto costume to join the dancing. Reserved seating sells out fast but standing areas are free. Last night features fireworks.

name: Awa Odori
highlights: Anyone can join the niwaka-ren groups
best_for: Participating in Japan's largest dance festival
travel_tip: Join a niwaka-ren (impromptu dance group) for free. Paid seating at Minami-Uchimachi is worth it for prime viewing. Arrive by 6 PM.

name: Tenjin Matsuri
best_for: Seeing fireworks reflected on the river alongside a boat procession
travel_tip: Arrive early afternoon to see the land procession before the evening river events. Spots along Sakuranomiya Park fill fast.

name: Kanda Matsuri
highlights: One of Tokyo's three great festivals
best_for: Experiencing Edo-era festival culture in the heart of modern Tokyo
travel_tip: Held in odd-numbered years only (full scale). The Saturday parade through Akihabara is the most photogenic. Arrive by 9 AM.

name: Sapporo Snow Festival
best_for: Seeing incredible snow art and experiencing Hokkaido winter culture
travel_tip: Book hotels months in advance. Odori Park site has the biggest sculptures. Susukino ice sculptures are stunning at night. Dress for -10C.

name: Takayama Spring Festival
highlights: Ornate yatai floats with mechanical puppets
best_for: Seeing centuries-old mechanical puppet performances on ornate floats
travel_tip: The evening parade on the first night with lantern-lit floats is unforgettable. Takayama is small; book ryokan early.

name: Takayama Autumn Festival
best_for: Experiencing the autumn counterpart with fewer crowds than spring
travel_tip: Slightly fewer tourists than the spring edition. Combine with autumn leaf viewing in the Hida region.

name: Kishiwada Danjiri Matsuri
best_for: Adrenaline seekers who want to see Japan's most dangerous festival
travel_tip: The yarimawashi (sharp corner turns) at Kankan-ba intersection is the highlight. Get there by 8 AM for a good spot.

name: Chichibu Night Festival
highlights: Massive floats hauled up steep slopes
best_for: Experiencing one of Japan's three great float festivals with winter fireworks
travel_tip: December 3 is the main night. Trains from Ikebukuro take about 80 minutes. Last trains fill up; consider staying overnight.

name: Kanamara Matsuri
best_for: Experiencing one of Japan's most unusual and open-minded festivals
travel_tip: Held on the first Sunday of April. Arrive early as streets get very crowded. The parade starts around 12 PM from Kanayama Shrine.

name: Nachi no Hi Matsuri
highlights: 12 giant torches carried down stone steps
best_for: Witnessing an ancient fire ritual at Japan's tallest waterfall
travel_tip: Remote location requires planning. Stay in Katsuura or Shingu the night before. The ritual starts at 2 PM on July 14.

name: Owara Kaze no Bon
highlights: Haunting bon dance with straw hats
best_for: Experiencing the most beautiful and atmospheric dance festival in Japan
travel_tip: Skip September 1-3 (main dates with huge crowds) and visit the pre-festival rehearsals in late August for a more intimate experience.

name: Hakata Gion Yamakasa
best_for: Watching raw athletic power as teams race through streets with massive floats
travel_tip: The Oiyama finale on July 15 starts at 4:59 AM. Yes, before dawn. Get there by 4 AM. The decorative kazari-yama floats are displayed from July 1.

name: Sumida River Fireworks
highlights: Two launch sites for continuous viewing
best_for: Experiencing Tokyo's biggest and most historic fireworks display
travel_tip: Over 1 million people attend. Arrive 3-4 hours early for a riverside spot. Sky Tree observation deck tickets sell out months ahead.

name: Sendai Tanabata Matsuri
highlights: Shopping arcades transformed with tanabata art
best_for: Strolling through tunnels of colorful handcrafted paper decorations
travel_tip: The fireworks on August 5 (eve) are worth seeing. Walk the full length of the covered arcades to see all the different streamer designs.

name: Toka Ebisu
best_for: Starting the new year with a traditional prayer for prosperity
travel_tip: January 9-11 at Imamiya Ebisu Shrine. January 10 (Hon Ebisu) is busiest. Buy a fuku-zasa (lucky bamboo) and add charms to it.

name: Setsubun
highlights: Geiko and maiko perform at Yasaka Shrine
Drive away evil spirits for spring
best_for: Participating in the symbolic transition from winter to spring
travel_tip: Celebrated nationwide on February 3. Yasaka Shrine and Yoshida Shrine in Kyoto have the most spectacular ceremonies. Eat ehomaki (lucky sushi roll).

name: Ueno Sakura Matsuri
highlights: 800+ cherry trees along the main path
best_for: Classic Tokyo hanami experience under hundreds of cherry trees
travel_tip: Peak bloom varies yearly (late March to early April). Weekday visits are far less crowded. Evening illumination creates a magical atmosphere.

name: Meguro River Cherry Blossoms
best_for: Combining cherry blossom viewing with a stylish Tokyo neighborhood walk
travel_tip: The stretch between Nakameguro and Ikejiri-Ohashi stations is the most scenic. Visit on a weekday evening for illuminated blossoms with fewer crowds.

name: Gozan no Okuribi (Daimonji)
highlights: Famous dai (large) character visible across Kyoto
best_for: Witnessing the spiritual send-off of ancestral spirits through mountain bonfires
travel_tip: August 16, starting at 8 PM. The Kamogawa riverbanks and Yoshida-yama offer free viewing. Fires last only about 30 minutes total.

name: Jidai Matsuri
highlights: Parade spans 1000 years of Kyoto history
One of Kyoto's three great festivals
best_for: Seeing Japanese history come alive through a living timeline parade
travel_tip: October 22. The parade starts at Kyoto Imperial Palace around noon and reaches Heian Shrine by 2:30 PM. Free standing; paid seats available.

name: Shichi-Go-San
best_for: Seeing adorable children in kimono at shrines throughout Japan
travel_tip: November 15 is the traditional date but families visit throughout November. Meiji Shrine and Hie Shrine in Tokyo are popular spots.

name: Aoi Matsuri
highlights: One of Kyoto's three great festivals
best_for: Stepping back into 6th-century Japan through an elegant imperial procession
travel_tip: May 15. The procession goes from Kyoto Imperial Palace to Shimogamo Shrine then Kamigamo Shrine. Paid seats at the palace offer the best views.

name: Sanja Matsuri
best_for: Feeling the raw energy of Tokyo's most exciting portable shrine festival
travel_tip: Third weekend of May. Sunday morning is the main event with three large mikoshi. The streets around Senso-ji become a massive party.

name: Otaru Snow Light Path
highlights: Hundreds of snow lanterns along the canal
Smaller and more intimate than Sapporo
best_for: A romantic winter evening strolling along a candlelit canal
travel_tip: Runs concurrently with Sapporo Snow Festival. Take the 30-minute train from Sapporo for a completely different atmosphere. Best after 5 PM.

name: Nagasaki Kunchi
best_for: Seeing Japan's most internationally influenced festival blending Chinese and European elements
travel_tip: October 7-9 at Suwa Shrine. The dragon dance (ja-odori) shows Chinese influence unique to Nagasaki. Reserved seats sell out; free standing areas available.

name: Koenji Awa Odori
highlights: Easier to attend than Tokushima original
best_for: Experiencing Awa Odori without traveling to Shikoku
travel_tip: Last weekend of August. The shopping streets transform into dance stages. Less crowded than Tokushima but equally energetic.

name: Kamakura Snow Hut Festival
highlights: Amazake and mochi served in snow huts
best_for: Sitting inside a snow hut and sharing warm amazake with locals
travel_tip: February 15-16. The miniature kamakura field near the river is incredibly photogenic at night. Dress very warmly.

name: Nada no Kenka Matsuri
best_for: Witnessing Japan's most intense fighting festival
travel_tip: October 14-15 at Matsubara Hachiman Shrine. The yatai crashing starts in the afternoon. Keep a safe distance from the action.

name: Tsuchiura All Japan Fireworks Competition
name_ja: 土浦全国花火競技大会
highlights: One of Japan's three great fireworks competitions
best_for: Seeing the absolute best in Japanese fireworks artistry
travel_tip: Usually first Saturday of October. Reserved seats sell out instantly. Free areas along the Sakura River fill up by early afternoon.

name: Nagaoka Fireworks Festival
name_ja: 長岡まつり大花火大会
highlights: Memorial for WWII bombing and earthquake
One of Japan's three great fireworks
best_for: Experiencing the emotional power of Japan's most moving fireworks display
travel_tip: August 2-3. The Phoenix fireworks are a memorial; the crowd falls silent then erupts. Book Shinkansen and hotels far in advance.

name: Mitama Matsuri
best_for: Walking through thousands of glowing lanterns on a warm summer night
travel_tip: July 13-16 at Yasukuni Shrine. The lanterns create an unforgettable atmosphere after dark. Evening visit recommended.

name: Nikko Toshogu Grand Spring Festival
name_ja: 日光東照宮春季例大祭
best_for: Seeing a massive samurai procession at one of Japan's most ornate shrines
travel_tip: May 17-18. The samurai procession on May 18 is the highlight. Combine with visiting the elaborate Toshogu shrine buildings.

name: Yosakoi Matsuri
highlights: Modern fusion of traditional and contemporary
best_for: Seeing explosive dance energy with creative modern choreography
travel_tip: August 9-12. Each team has unique costumes and choreography. Otesuji competition venue has the best atmosphere.

name: Yamaga Lantern Festival
highlights: Women dance with golden paper lanterns on heads
best_for: Watching a mesmerizing dance of golden lanterns in the night
travel_tip: August 15-16. The Senbon-toro (thousand lantern dance) on August 16 night is the highlight. Bus from Kumamoto Station (about 1 hour).
